subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which nerve supplies the anterior wall and roof of the external auditory canal (EAC)?
back: Auriculotemporal nerve (branch of V3)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which nerves supply the posterior wall and floor of the EAC?
back: CN VII and CN X

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: The posterior part of the pinna above the concha is supplied by which nerve (not the greater occipital nerve)?
back: Lesser occipital nerve (C2)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which nerve supplies the lower posterior pinna/lobule area (great auricular nerve)?
back: Great auricular nerve (C2, C3)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which cranial nerve supplies the middle ear via Jacobson's nerve?
back: CN IX (glossopharyngeal) - Jacobson's nerve supplies the middle ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which nerve supplies the utricle, superior semicircular canal, and lateral semicircular canal?
back: Superior vestibular nerve

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which nerve supplies the saccule and posterior semicircular canal?
back: Inferior vestibular nerve

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In referred otalgia, tonsillitis refers pain via which cranial nerve?
back: CN IX (glossopharyngeal)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In referred otalgia, laryngeal/pharyngeal pathology refers pain via which cranial nerve?
back: CN X (vagus)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In referred otalgia, facial pathology refers pain via which nerve?
back: V3 (mandibular division of trigeminal nerve)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the total length of the external auditory canal (EAC)?
back: 24 mm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where is the narrowest part of the EAC located?
back: 5 mm lateral to the tympanic membrane (isthmus)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What type of tissue makes up the outer 1/3 of the EAC, and what fissures/communication does it have?
back: Cartilaginous outer 1/3, containing the fissures of Santorini, which communicate with the parotid gland

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What type of tissue makes up the inner 2/3 of the EAC, and what foramen/communication does it have?
back: Bony inner 2/3, containing the foramen of Hushke (present in children <4 years), which communicates with the skull base

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In which direction should ear syringing be directed?
back: Upward - Outward - Backward

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Syncope during ear syringing is due to stimulation of which nerve?
back: Arnold's nerve (auricular branch of vagus, CN X)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Hitzelberger sign and what does it indicate?
back: Anaesthesia of the posterior wall/floor of the EAC due to CN VII involvement; classically seen in acoustic (vestibular) neuroma

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: The incisura terminalis is associated with which surgical incision?
back: Lempert endaural incision

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: By what age does the mastoid tip fully develop?
back: By ≥2 years of age

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: The pinna develops from fusion of which embryological structures?
back: Hillocks of His (from the 1st and 2nd branchial arches), which fuse together

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where is a pre-auricular sinus classically located?
back: At the root of the helix

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In which two parts of the pinna is cartilage absent?
back: Incisura (terminalis) and the lobule

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Darwin's tubercle, developmentally speaking?
back: An atavistic feature of the pinna

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What condition is also known as cauliflower ear or boxer's ear, and how is it treated?
back: Perichondrial hematoma of the pinna; treated by Incision & Drainage (I&D)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the key features of malignant otitis externa (MOE)?
back: History of diabetes; associated with 7th cranial nerve (facial) palsy; caused by Pseudomonas

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the drug of choice (DOC) for malignant otitis externa?
back: Ceftazidime / Cefoperazone

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which imaging investigation is used to diagnose malignant otitis externa, and which is used to monitor treatment response/follow-up?
back: Diagnosis: Tc99 (Technetium-99) bone scan; Follow-up response: Gallium scan

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is keratosis obliterans characterized by, on the EAC epithelium?
back: Desquamative epithelium (build-up of keratin debris obliterating the canal)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What condition is classically called 'surfer's ear'?
back: Exostosis of the EAC

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What condition is classically called 'swimmer's ear', and what are its features?
back: Generalised/diffuse otitis externa; most common cause is Pseudomonas; presents with severe pain and tenderness (pain++, tender++)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the 'wet newspaper sign' and which organism causes it?
back: A sign of otomycosis, caused by Aspergillus niger

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the angle and vibratory area of the tympanic membrane (TM)?
back: 55 degrees; 55 mm² vibratory area

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the three histological layers of the tympanic membrane?
back: Epithelial layer, Fibrous layer, Mucosal layer

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: The pars flaccida of the TM is also known by what name?
back: Shrapnell's membrane

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the areal ratio of the TM to the stapes footplate, and what is its significance?
back: 17 (TM area : stapes footplate area) - contributes to sound amplification

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the lever ratio of the ossicular chain, and what is its function?
back: 1.3 - provides amplifying force

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the total transformer ratio of the middle ear (total amplification)?

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the phase difference between the oval window and round window, and why does it matter?
back: +4 dB - it prevents sound cancellation between the two windows and aids in the transformer mechanism

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What structures are contained in the epitympanum, and what is its width?
back: Pars flaccida, Prussak's space, and the scutum; width is 6 mm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which bone is the first to be eroded in primary (attic) cholesteatoma?
back: The scutum

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does the mesotympanum contain, and what is its narrowest width?
back: Contains the pars tensa; narrowest space, 2 mm wide

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the width of the hypotympanum, and how does it compare to the other middle ear spaces?
back: 4 mm wide - the smallest space of the middle ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: On otoscopy, where is the cone of light located in the right ear vs the left ear?
back: Right ear: 5 o'clock position; Left ear: 7 o'clock position

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In acute suppurative otitis media (ASOM), what is the typical shape and site of TM perforation?
back: Curvilinear shape, at the postero-inferior quadrant (PIQ)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which quadrant of the TM is the most common site for spontaneous perforation, myringotomy, and grommet insertion?
back: Antero-inferior quadrant (AIQ)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In middle ear anatomy, the promontory overlies which structure?
back: The basal turn of the cochlea

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does 'ET' refer to in middle ear anatomy diagrams?
back: The Eustachian tube (pharyngotympanic tube)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Canal of Hughier associated with?
back: The course of the chorda tympani nerve/tympanic branch of the glossopharyngeal nerve (Jacobson's nerve) region of the middle ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which structure is the most common site for residual cholesteatoma in the middle ear?
back: The sinus tympani

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Cleft palate predisposes to which recurrent ear condition, and why?
back: Recurrent ear infection (otitis media), due to Eustachian tube dysfunction

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How does the Eustachian tube differ in children compared to adults, and what condition does this predispose to?
back: In children the Eustachian tube is shorter, wider, and more horizontal, which predisposes to otitis media

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Ostmann's pad of fat, and what condition results from its dysfunction/atrophy?
back: A fat pad related to the Eustachian tube; its dysfunction leads to a patulous Eustachian tube, causing autophony

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Name the boundaries/contents visualized in the right middle ear view given in the notes.
back: Prussak's space, cochleariform process, tensor tympani (TT), long arm of incus, umbo, Eustachian tube (ET), Jacobson's nerve, promontory, round window (RW) niche, stapedial ligament, pyramidal eminence, ponticulus, sinus tympani, subiculum, posterior sinus, subtympanic sinus, lenticular process

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which part of the ossicular chain is most commonly eroded in cholesteatoma?
back: The long process of the incus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the parts of the stapes?
back: Head, anterior process, and handle-equivalent structures leading to the footplate; connected via the lenticular process to the incus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the parts of the malleus and incus relevant to ossicular anatomy?
back: Malleus: head, handle; Incus: body, long process, lenticular process

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is MacEwen's triangle and how deep is the mastoid antrum located within it?
back: A surgical landmark for locating the mastoid antrum; the antrum lies 1.5 cm deep to it

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the three boundaries of MacEwen's triangle?
back: Dural plate/supramastoid crest, Spine of Henle (posterosuperior to the EAC), and the tangential line

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the facial recess, and what structures border it?
back: A middle ear space bordered by structures including the incus (I), lateral semicircular canal (LSC), and facial nerve (FN); it lies near the sigmoid sinus and is a surgical approach route (posterior tympanotomy)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which radiographic view is used to visualize mastoid pneumatic cells?
back: Law's view / Schuller's view

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Citelli's subdural angle, and on which view is it identified?
back: An angle formed between the dural plate and sigmoid sinus, identified on Law's/Schuller's view; landmarks include the mandibular condyle, EAM, dural plate, sigmoid sinus, and mastoid air cells

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the three boundaries of Trautmann's triangle, and what does it represent?
back: Represents the posterior cranial fossa; Superior boundary: superior petrosal sinus; Posterior boundary: sigmoid sinus; Anterior boundary: bony labyrinth

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Korner septum, and what surgical problem does it cause?
back: A persistent petrosquamous suture; it can lead to incomplete clearance of mastoid air cells during mastoid surgery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How many turns does the cochlea have?
back: 2¾ (2 and 3/4) turns

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the bony centre of the cochlea called?
back: The modiolus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What function do the cristae in the semicircular canals (SCC) serve?
back: Dynamic/rotational stability

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What function do the maculae in the utricle/saccule serve?
back: Linear stability

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which ion predominates in endolymph, and where is endolymph produced?
back: K+ (potassium) predominant; produced by the stria vascularis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where is endolymph absorbed?
back: In the endolymphatic sac, via the vestibular aqueduct

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which ion predominates in perilymph?
back: Na+ (sodium) predominant

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: The cochlear aqueduct connects the inner ear to which fluid space, and what complication can result from infection there?
back: Connects to CSF; meningitis can spread to cause labyrinthitis via the cochlear aqueduct

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is the utricle oriented, and how is the saccule oriented?
back: Utricle: horizontal orientation; Saccule: vertical orientation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where in the cochlea are low frequency sounds perceived, and what structure is located there?
back: At the apical turn of the cochlea, at the helicotrema

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where in the cochlea are high frequency sounds perceived, and what structure is located there?
back: At the basal turn of the cochlea, at the promontory

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How many rows of outer hair cells (OHC) are there, and what is their function?
back: 3 rows; they modulate/amplify cochlear input

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How many rows of inner hair cells (IHC) are there, and what is their function?
back: Single row; responsible for transmission (main sensory transducers of sound)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Hair cells transduce mechanical energy into an electrical signal using which neurotransmitter?
back: Glutamate

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Afferent fibers of the cochlear nerve arise from which ganglion?
back: The spiral ganglion

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Glue ear (serous otitis media) in an elderly patient should raise suspicion for what underlying condition?
back: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NP Ca) - must be ruled out

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Glue ear (serous otitis media) in a young child is most commonly caused by what?
back: Adenoid hypertrophy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Bullous myringitis presents with what symptom, and what organisms cause it?
back: Significant pain (pain+); caused by HSV or Mycoplasma

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which organisms most commonly cause acute otitis media (AOM)?
back: Streptococcus pneumoniae is more common than non-typable Haemophilus influenzae

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What type of ear discharge is seen in TB otitis media, and what must be ruled out?
back: Painless otorrhoea; perforation risk is increased, so perforation must be ruled out

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the four clinical/radiological signs associated with glomus tumours (paragangliomas) of the ear?
back: Rising sun sign, Aquino sign, Brown sign, Phelp sign (plus Fisch staging system)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Aquino sign in glomus tumour evaluation?
back: Blanching of the tumour on occlusion of the internal carotid artery (ICA)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Brown sign in glomus tumour evaluation?
back: Blanching of the tumour seen with Siegel's pneumatic speculum

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Phelp sign in glomus tumour evaluation?
back: Erosions of the jugular spine

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What staging system is used for glomus tumours (paragangliomas)?
back: Fisch staging

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the four stages of acute otitis media (AOM) progression?
back: 1) Obstruction, 2) Pre-suppuration (Cartwheel sign), 3) Suppuration (Lighthouse sign - maximum pain), 4) Resolution (perforation or resolves with antibiotics)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the 'Cartwheel sign' seen in AOM, and at which stage?
back: A sign seen during the pre-suppuration stage of acute otitis media

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the 'Lighthouse sign' seen in AOM, and what does it signify?
back: A sign seen during the suppuration stage of AOM, corresponding to maximum pain

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Cholesteatoma is classically described by what phrase?
back: 'Skin in the wrong place'

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the mechanism/theory behind primary cholesteatoma?
back: Eustachian tube dysfunction leading to a retraction pocket (Wittmaack's theory)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the mechanism/theory behind secondary cholesteatoma?
back: Perforation - central or pars tensa perforation (Habermann's theory)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the mechanism/theory behind congenital cholesteatoma?
back: Congenital epithelial cell rests (Levenson's theory)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which grading system is used for pars tensa (PT) retraction pockets, and which for pars flaccida (PF) retraction pockets?
back: Pars tensa (PT): Sade's grading; Pars flaccida (PF): Tos grading

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the grades of Sade's classification for pars tensa retraction?
back: Grade I: slight retraction of the pars tensa; Grade II: retraction onto the incus/stapes; Grade III: retraction onto the promontory, not adherent; Grade IV: retraction onto the promontory, adherent; Grade V: perforation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic otoscopic appearance of cholesteatoma?
back: A 'pearly white mass'

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the two clinical types of chronic suppurative otitis media (CSOM)?
back: Tubotympanic type ('safe' type) and Atticoantral type ('unsafe' type)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the etiology of tubotympanic type CSOM, and which part of the TM is involved?
back: Recurrent acute otitis media; involves the pars tensa ('PT')

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the etiology of atticoantral type CSOM, and which part of the TM is involved?
back: Retraction pocket cholesteatoma with marginal/attic perforation; involves the pars flaccida ('PF')

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Describe the ear discharge in tubotympanic vs atticoantral CSOM.
back: Tubotympanic: profuse, mucoid, non-foul smelling discharge. Atticoantral: scanty, purulent, foul-smelling discharge

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Is granulation tissue/polyp present in tubotympanic vs atticoantral CSOM, and what is a key management rule?
back: Tubotympanic: absent. Atticoantral: present (++). Key rule: never avulse a polyp (it may be attached to a vital underlying structure)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Compare the complication risk between tubotympanic and atticoantral CSOM.
back: Tubotympanic type has low risk of complications; atticoantral type has high risk, including bony erosion

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In atticoantral CSOM, which ossicle is eroded earliest, and which is most commonly eroded?
back: Earliest eroded: the scutum. Most commonly (MC) eroded: the long process of the incus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: The Austin classification of ossicular erosion is based on the status of which structures?
back: The handle of the malleus and the stapes suprastructure

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the two major categories of complications of atticoantral CSOM?
back: Facial nerve palsy and intracranial complications

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the earliest sign of mastoiditis?
back: An 'ironed out' appearance of the mastoid, and the reservoir sign

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the most common site of soft tissue (subperiosteal) abscess formation in mastoiditis?
back: Retro-auricular region

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Along which muscle does a Bezold's abscess track?
back: Sternocleidomastoid (SCM)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Along which muscle does a Citelli's abscess track?
back: Digastric muscle

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where does a Luc's abscess track to?
back: Into the external auditory canal (EAC)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: On imaging of lateral sinus thrombosis, how does the thrombus appear on NCCT vs CECT?
back: NCCT: hyperdense; CECT: filling defect

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a positive Griesinger sign indicate?
back: Lateral sinus / transverse sinus thrombosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does the Tobey-Ayer test assess, and how is it performed?
back: Assesses for lateral sinus thrombosis; shows increased CSF pressure upon compressing the internal jugular vein (IJV)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Along with the Griesinger sign and Tobey-Ayer test, what other test/sign is associated with lateral sinus thrombosis in the notes?
back: Crowe-Beck test, and the 'picket fence fever' pattern

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Gradenigo syndrome, and what is its underlying cause?
back: Caused by apical petrositis; presents as a triad of ear discharge, diplopia (CN VI palsy), and retro-orbital pain (CN V palsy)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Wullstein's Type I tympanoplasty, and what is another name for it?
back: Also called myringoplasty; used for non-healing perforation (≥3 months); the TM graft (temporalis fascia) is placed over an intact malleus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Wullstein's Type II tympanoplasty?
back: TM is grafted over the incus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Wullstein's Type III tympanoplasty, and what is it also called?
back: Also called columella tympanoplasty / myringostapediopexy; TM is grafted directly over the stapes

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Wullstein's Type IV tympanoplasty?
back: Round window shielding / cavum minor technique, where the oval window is left exposed

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Wullstein's Type V tympanoplasty?
back: Fenestration - creation of an opening in the lateral semicircular canal (SCC)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What graft material is classically used in Type I tympanoplasty (myringoplasty)?
back: Temporalis fascia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the difference between a PORP and a TORP ossicular prosthesis?
back: PORP (Partial Ossicular Replacement Prosthesis) connects the malleus/incus to an intact stapes suprastructure; TORP (Total Ossicular Replacement Prosthesis) is used when only the stapes footplate remains

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does 'Canal Wall Up' mastoid surgery correspond to, and what surgical step does it involve?
back: Corresponds to posterior tympanotomy; the posterior wall of the EAC is preserved

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the advantages/disadvantages of Canal Wall Up surgery?
back: Preserves the posterior wall of the EAC, but has a high rate of residual disease and recurrence

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does 'Canal Wall Down' mastoid surgery include, and what structures are preserved in each variant?
back: Includes Radical Mastoidectomy (stapes footplate preserved) and Modified Radical Mastoidectomy/MRM (TM and ossicles preserved)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the disadvantages of Canal Wall Down surgery?
back: The posterior wall of the EAC is not preserved (creates a common cavity), leading to poor water tolerance and difficulty fitting a hearing aid

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the indications for Simple Cortical Mastoidectomy (Schwartz operation)?
back: Acute coalescent or masked mastoiditis; acute otitis media with reservoir sign; as an initial step before endolymphatic sac surgery or facial nerve decompression

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the indications for Radical Mastoidectomy?
back: Residual cholesteatoma, glomus tumour, and carcinoma of the middle ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In microtia/anotia, what material is used for pinna reconstruction, and at what minimum age is it performed?
back: Costal cartilage; performed at ≥6 years of age

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which three tuning fork frequencies are used in hearing assessment?
back: 256 Hz, 512 Hz, and 1024 Hz

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the approximate air-bone gap corresponding to each tuning fork frequency (256/512/1024 Hz)?

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a positive Rinne's test indicate, and what is the finding?
back: Air conduction (AC) greater than bone conduction (BC); indicates normal hearing or sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a negative Rinne's test indicate, and what is the finding?
back: Bone conduction (BC) greater than air conduction (AC); indicates conductive hearing loss (CHL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is a 'false negative' Rinne's test, and when does it occur?
back: A negative Rinne's result occurring due to severe unilateral SNHL (the contralateral ear picks up the bone-conducted sound)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In Weber's test, what does lateralization to the same (ipsilateral) ear indicate?
back: Conductive hearing loss (CHL) in that ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In Weber's test, what does lateralization to the contralateral (opposite) ear indicate?
back: Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) in the ear away from which sound is lateralized

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What findings are seen with reduced ABC (absolute bone conduction) and reduced Schwabach test?
back: Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: A negative Bing test and a negative Gelle's test indicate what type of hearing loss?
back: Conductive hearing loss (CHL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic Gelle's test finding in otosclerosis?
back: Gelle's test is negative in otosclerosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the WHO grading of hearing impairment, what dBHL range and performance level defines Grade 0?
back: ≤25 dBHL; no/very slight problem, able to hear a whisper at 1 metre

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the WHO grading of hearing impairment, what dBHL range and performance level defines Grade 1?
back: 26-40 dBHL; slight impairment, able to hear and repeat words in a normal voice at 1 metre

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the WHO grading of hearing impairment, what dBHL range and performance level defines Grade 2?
back: 41-60 dBHL; moderate impairment, able to hear and repeat words using a raised voice at 1 metre

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the WHO grading of hearing impairment, what dBHL range and performance level defines Grade 3?
back: 61-80 dBHL; severe impairment, able to hear some words when shouted into the better ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the WHO grading of hearing impairment, what dBHL range and performance level defines Grade 4?
back: ≥81 dBHL; profound impairment including deafness, unable to hear and understand even shouted voice

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What degree of hearing loss is typically caused by a TM perforation?

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the maximum conductive hearing loss caused by complete obstruction of the EAC (as simulated by headphones)?
back: 40 dB

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What degree of hearing loss occurs with ossicular discontinuity when the TM is intact?
back: 55 dB

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What degree of hearing loss occurs with complete stapes footplate fixation, and what is its significance?
back: 60 dB - this represents the maximum possible conductive hearing loss

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the tolerable sound level for the human ear?
back: 90 dB

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: At what sound intensity does TM rupture typically occur?
back: 150 dB

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: On a pure tone audiogram (PTA), what colour convention is used for the right ear and the left ear?
back: Right ear (Rt): red; Left ear (Lt): blue

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What audiogram pattern characterizes presbycusis?
back: High frequency hearing loss with a falling curve, typically in the elderly

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Carhart's notch, and in which condition is it seen?
back: A dip in bone conduction thresholds (classically around 2000 Hz) seen in otosclerosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is paracusis (Willisii), and in which condition is it seen?
back: Better hearing ability in noisy/loud environments; seen in otosclerosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How does pregnancy affect otosclerosis?
back: It can worsen/accelerate otosclerosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the typical hearing loss pattern in otosclerosis?
back: Bilateral, gradual conductive hearing loss (CHL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the fissula ante fenestrum in otosclerosis?
back: The classic anatomical site of the otosclerotic focus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Von der Hoeve syndrome?
back: The association of osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) with otosclerosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Schwartz sign, and what does it indicate?
back: A salmon-pink appearance of the tympanic membrane, indicating active otosclerosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What medical treatment is used for active otosclerosis, and what is its key contraindication?
back: Sodium fluoride (NaF); contraindicated in pregnancy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the definitive surgical treatment sequence for otosclerosis?
back: Stapedotomy, escalating to stapedectomy if needed

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What audiogram pattern is seen in Meniere's disease/endolymphatic hydrops?
back: Low frequency hearing loss

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What audiogram pattern is described as 'cookie-bite' or U-shaped, and what condition does it indicate?
back: Mid-frequency hearing loss pattern, seen in congenital hearing loss

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Boiler's notch, and at what frequency does it occur? What condition causes it?
back: A notch/dip at 4000 Hz on the audiogram, seen in noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic symptom triad of Meniere's disease, and how does it present in laterality/timing?
back: Unilateral (U/L) episodic Tinnitus, Vertigo, and Sensorineural hearing loss (TVS triad)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Lermoyez syndrome?
back: An atypical presentation of Meniere's disease where sensorineural hearing loss improves as the vertigo attack occurs (SNHL --> Vertigo)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is diplacusis, and in which condition is it seen?
back: Perceiving the same tone/pitch differently between the two ears; seen in Meniere's disease

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Tulio phenomenon?
back: Vertigo triggered by loud sound

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Tumarkin's otolithic crisis?
back: Sudden drop attacks, seen in Meniere's disease

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for Meniere's disease, and what is the diagnostic threshold?
back: Electrocochleography (ECoG); an SP/AP ratio >45% supports the diagnosis of endolymphatic hydrops

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the general treatment approach for Meniere's disease?
back: Avoidance of caffeine, alcohol, and antihistamines; plus steroids

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Meniett device used for?
back: Treatment of Meniere's disease (delivers low-pressure pulses to the middle ear)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Donaldson's line used for?
back: To locate the endolymphatic sac during endolymphatic sac decompression surgery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Silverstein microwick used for?
back: To deliver intratympanic gentamicin to the scala tympani in the treatment of Meniere's disease

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What SP/AP ratio value on ECoG is considered normal (as shown for congenital HL context)?
back: SP/AP ratio <30%

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which test is the investigation of choice (IOC) for conductive hearing loss (CHL)?
back: Impedance audiometry (tympanometry with stapedial reflex testing)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a Type A tympanometry curve indicate?
back: Normal middle ear function

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a Type Ad tympanometry curve indicate?
back: Ossicular discontinuity

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a Type As tympanometry curve indicate?
back: Otosclerosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a Type B tympanometry curve indicate?
back: Serous otitis media

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does a Type C tympanometry curve indicate?
back: Eustachian tube dysfunction / retracted tympanic membrane

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: On speech audiometry, what findings suggest a cochlear lesion (e.g., Meniere's disease)?
back: Abnormally high SISI score; presence of recruitment on ABLB testing (abnormal growth of perceived loudness with increasing sound intensity)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: On speech audiometry, what findings suggest a retrocochlear lesion (e.g., 8th nerve/vestibular schwannoma)?
back: Roll-over phenomenon; poor speech discrimination; presence of tone decay; abnormal stapedial reflex; BERA is the investigation of choice

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What type of hearing loss does superior canal dehiscence classically cause?
back: Conductive hearing loss (CHL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the function of the stapedial reflex, and what does it prevent?
back: Dampens loud noise and prevents hyperacusis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the afferent pathway of the stapedial reflex?
back: CN VIII (ipsilateral, I/L)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the efferent pathway of the stapedial reflex?
back: CN VII (bilateral, B/L), via the superior olive

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does OAE (otoacoustic emissions) testing assess, and what is it primarily used for?
back: Assesses outer hair cell (OHC) function; used as a newborn hearing screening test

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Describe the pathway of a transient evoked OAE.
back: Outer hair cells (OHC) -> basilar membrane -> perilymph -> oval window (OW) -> ossicles -> TM -> EAC

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which test is used as the confirmatory hearing test after an abnormal OAE screening?
back: BERA (Brainstem Evoked Response Audiometry)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the newborn hearing screening timeline, by when should screening, confirmation, and rehabilitation occur?
back: Screening by 1 month (in all babies); Confirmation by 3 months; Rehabilitation by 6 months

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the minimum age for cochlear implantation?
back: 9 months

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Using the BERA wave mnemonic 'E-E-C-O-L-I-M-A', which anatomical structures correspond to the successive waves?
back: 8th cranial nerve, cochlear nucleus, superior olive, lateral lemniscus, inferior colliculus, medial geniculate body (MGB), and auditory cortex (Area 41)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which class of drugs are grouped together as major ototoxic drugs?
back: Aminoglycosides, Vancomycin, Cisplatin, and Macrolides

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: The mnemonic 'GST' (Gentamicin, Streptomycin, Tobramycin) refers to drugs causing which type of ototoxicity?
back: Vestibulotoxicity

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which drugs cause ototoxicity by damaging the stria vascularis?
back: Diuretics (Ethacrynic acid more than loop diuretics) and Quinine

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the most common location of a vestibular schwannoma, and what is its classic imaging appearance?
back: Most common in the cerebellopontine angle (CPA); classic 'ice-cream cone' appearance

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: From which nerve does a vestibular schwannoma most commonly arise?
back: The inferior vestibular nerve (IVN)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for vestibular schwannoma?
back: Contrast-enhanced MRI (CE-MRI)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment approach for vestibular schwannoma based on size?
back: ≤3 cm: Gamma knife radiosurgery; >3 cm: Surgery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Vestibular schwannoma is associated with which genetic condition?
back: Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are Antoni A and Antoni B areas, and what characteristic structures are found in Antoni A areas?
back: Histological patterns seen in schwannoma; Verrucay (Verocay) bodies are found in Antoni A areas

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the typical latency of nystagmus in peripheral vertigo vs central vertigo?
back: Peripheral vertigo: 2-20 seconds latency; Central vertigo: no latency

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the typical duration of an episode in peripheral vertigo vs central vertigo?
back: Peripheral vertigo: less than 1 minute; Central vertigo: more than 1 minute

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How does the direction of nystagmus differ between peripheral and central vertigo?
back: Peripheral: direction is fixed, beating towards the undermost ear; Central: direction is changing

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Is nystagmus fatigable in peripheral vs central vertigo?
back: Peripheral vertigo: fatigable; Central vertigo: non-fatigable

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How severe are accompanying symptoms in peripheral vs central vertigo?
back: Peripheral vertigo has severe accompanying vertigo symptoms; central vertigo does not (typically milder/absent)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Is nystagmus suppressed by optic fixation in peripheral vs central vertigo?
back: Peripheral: yes, suppressed by optic fixation; Central: no, not suppressed

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Is nystagmus enhanced in darkness/with Frenzel glasses in peripheral vs central vertigo?
back: Peripheral: yes, enhanced; Central: no, not enhanced

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does the COWS mnemonic describe, and what does it mean?
back: Describes the direction of nystagmus fast phase during caloric testing - Cold water: nystagmus fast phase beats to the Opposite side; Warm water: nystagmus fast phase beats to the Same side

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Fitzgerald-Hallpike test also known as?
back: The bithermal caloric test

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which instrument is used to perform the fistula test for vertigo?
back: Siegel's pneumatic speculum

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the classic causes of a positive fistula test?
back: Erosion of the horizontal semicircular canal (SCC), fenestration surgery, and post-stapedectomy fistula

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What causes a false negative fistula test?
back: A dead labyrinth or cholesteatoma overlying the fistula

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What causes a false positive fistula test, and what is this sign called?
back: Meniere's disease or congenital syphilis (with an abnormally mobile stapes footplate); this is called the Hennebert sign

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does cVEMP (cervical VEMP) test, and what is its neural pathway?
back: Tests saccule function; pathway: Saccule -> Inferior Vestibular Nerve (IVN) -> vestibular nuclei -> ipsilateral vestibular spinal tract -> CN XI -> sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does oVEMP (ocular VEMP) test, and what is its neural pathway?
back: Tests utricle function; pathway: Utricle -> Superior Vestibular Nerve (SVN) -> vestibular nuclei -> MLF -> CN III -> inferior oblique muscle

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In which conditions is the VEMP response decreased?
back: Acoustic neuroma and vestibular neuritis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does the head impulse test assess, and what finding indicates a vestibular deficit?
back: Assesses the vestibulo-ocular reflex; presence of a corrective (compensatory) saccade after head movement indicates a peripheral vestibular deficit on that side

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is vertigo broadly categorized in the initial approach?
back: Category A: Only vertigo, with no hearing loss; Category B: Vertigo with hearing loss

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Under 'vertigo without hearing loss', what are the two main paroxysmal/positional and acute-onset differentials?
back: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV) - paroxysmal and positional; and Vestibular neuritis - acute onset

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the underlying pathology of BPPV, and which semicircular canal is most commonly involved?
back: Due to otolith debris (canalithiasis); most commonly involves the posterior semicircular canal

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which test is used to diagnose BPPV, and which manoeuver is used to treat it?
back: Diagnosis: Dix-Hallpike manoeuver; Treatment: Epley's manoeuver

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Under 'vertigo with hearing loss', what are the three main differentials listed?
back: Meniere's disease (TVS - Tinnitus, Vertigo, SNHL), Perilymphatic fistula, and Labyrinthitis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How does serous labyrinthitis affect vestibular function - hyperactive or hypoactive, and which side is affected?
back: Serous labyrinthitis is hyperactive, affecting the ipsilateral (I/L) side

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How does purulent labyrinthitis or traumatic labyrinthitis affect vestibular function - hyperactive or hypoactive, and which side is affected?
back: Purulent labyrinthitis/trauma is hypoactive, affecting the contralateral (C/L) side

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the HINTS examination used for, and what are its three components?
back: Used to differentiate central from peripheral causes of Acute Vestibular Syndrome; components are: Head Impulse test, Nystagmus pattern, and Test of Skew (vertical skew deviation)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In the HINTS exam, what finding on the Test of Skew suggests a central cause of acute vertigo?
back: A positive vertical skew deviation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the first-line treatment for hearing loss?
back: Hearing aids

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does BAHA stand for, and what are its external and implanted parts?
back: Bone Anchored Hearing Aid; external part includes the audio processor; implanted part includes the conductor link and floating mass transducer

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the key indication for a BAHA (osseo-integrated device)?
back: Unilateral hearing loss present for more than 5 years, using osseo-integration

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where is the auditory brainstem implant located?
back: In the 4th ventricle, at the lateral recess

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to William House criteria, who is a candidate for a cochlear implant?
back: A patient with bilateral severe to profound hearing loss not responding to hearing aids, who has an intact (functioning) auditory nerve

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Michel aplasia, and what is its management?
back: Complete cochlear aplasia; managed with a cochlear implant (CI)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Mondini dysplasia?
back: A cochlear malformation with only 1.5 turns instead of the normal 2¾ turns, affecting the basal turn of the cochlea

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Scheibe dysplasia, and how common is it?
back: The most common (MC) inner ear dysplasia; it is a cochleosaccular dysplasia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the definition of sudden sensorineural hearing loss (SSNHL)?
back: 30 dB or more of SNHL over at least three contiguous frequencies, occurring within a period of 3 days

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment for sudden SNHL?
back: Rest, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, and steroids

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the segments of the facial nerve (fallopian canal) from proximal to distal?
back: Meatal segment, Labyrinthine segment, Geniculate ganglion (1st genu), Tympanic/Horizontal segment, 2nd genu, Mastoid/Vertical segment, and the Stylomastoid foramen

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which segment of the facial nerve is the narrowest and shortest, and which infection most commonly affects it?
back: The labyrinthine segment; most commonly affected in viral infections (HSV-1), as in Bell's palsy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What landmark corresponds to the geniculate ganglion (1st genu) of the facial nerve?
back: The processus cochleariformis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which nerve branches off near the geniculate ganglion, and what function does it serve?
back: The greater superficial petrosal nerve (GSPN), which supplies lacrimation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which segment of the facial nerve is the most common site of bony dehiscence, and what middle ear wall does it form?
back: The tympanic/horizontal segment; it forms the medial wall of the middle ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What landmark corresponds to the 2nd genu of the facial nerve?
back: The pyramid (pyramidal eminence)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which segment of the facial nerve is the most common site of injury during mastoid surgery, and what middle ear wall does it form?
back: The mastoid/vertical segment; it forms the posterior wall of the middle ear

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What two branches arise from the mastoid (vertical) segment of the facial nerve, and what does each supply?
back: Nerve to stapedius (damage causes hyperacusis) and chorda tympani (carries taste from the anterior 2/3rd of the tongue and supplies the submandibular and sublingual glands)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where does the facial nerve exit the skull?
back: Through the stylomastoid foramen

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the nervus intermedius (nerve of Wrisberg), and what type of fibres does it carry?
back: A component of the facial nerve carrying sensory and secretomotor fibres

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How does a UMN (upper motor neuron) CN VII palsy differ from an LMN (lower motor neuron) CN VII palsy in terms of laterality?
back: UMN palsy affects the contralateral (C/L) side with forehead sparing; LMN palsy affects the ipsilateral (I/L) side, involving the forehead as well

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the clinical features of a lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve palsy?
back: Loss of forehead wrinkles, wide palpebral fissure, epiphora, absence of the nasolabial fold, and drooping of the angle of the mouth

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the four surgical landmarks used to identify the facial nerve trunk during parotid surgery?
back: Cartilaginous pointer (tragus), tympanomastoid suture, styloid process, and posterior belly of digastric

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the five terminal branches of the facial nerve in the parotid gland, from superior to inferior?
back: Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal Mandibular, and Cervical branches

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: If the facial nerve is injured intra-operatively, how is it repaired?
back: By cable grafting using the great auricular nerve (GAN)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Bogorad syndrome, and what is its alternate name?
back: Also called crocodile tears syndrome; presents with gustatory lacrimation (tearing while eating)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Ramsay Hunt syndrome, and what is another name for it?
back: Also called Herpes Zoster Oticus; presents with Bell's palsy plus vesicles on the EAC/pinna

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome associated with?
back: Bell's palsy plus a fissured tongue

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which X-ray views are best for visualizing the maxillary sinus?
back: Modified Water's view (also called Pierre's view)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which X-ray views are best for visualizing the frontal sinus?
back: Caldwell view (also called Occipito-frontal view)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Towne's view used to visualize?
back: The foramen magnum and sella turcica

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for paranasal sinus (PNS) disease?
back: HRCT (High Resolution CT)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the gold-standard surgical treatment for chronic sinus disease, and what is its first step?
back: FESS (Functional Endoscopic Sinus Surgery); the first step is uncinectomy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where does the nasolacrimal duct drain into?
back: The inferior meatus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which sinuses/structures drain into the middle meatus (per the FRAME mnemonic), and what surgical opening is also made here?
back: The frontal, anterior ethmoid, and maxillary sinuses drain into the middle meatus (FRAME); the DCR (dacryocystorhinostomy) opening is also created here

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where does the posterior ethmoid sinus drain?
back: Into the superior meatus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Where does the sphenoid sinus drain?
back: Into the spheno-ethmoidal (SE) recess

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which ethmoidal air cell is the anterior-most?
back: The agger nasi cell

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which ethmoidal air cell is the largest?
back: The bulla ethmoidalis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which ethmoidal air cell is infra-orbital in location, and what can it cause if enlarged?
back: The Haller cell; it can block the maxillary sinus ostium

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which ethmoidal air cell, if present, can compress the optic nerve, and where is it located?
back: The Onodi cell; it is the posterior-most ethmoid air cell

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which anterior ethmoid air cell extends into the frontal sinus, and what condition can it predispose to?
back: The Kuhn cell (fronto-ethmoidal cell); it can predispose to frontal sinusitis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: On nasal endoscopy, how does a hypertrophied turbinate appear compared to a concha bullosa?
back: Hypertrophied turbinate: irregular surface; Concha (bullosa): smooth surface

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: During diagnostic nasal endoscopy, what structures are visualized on the 'first pass'?
back: The inferior meatus, leading to the nasolacrimal duct (NLD)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: During diagnostic nasal endoscopy, what structures are visualized on the 'second pass'?
back: The middle meatus lateral wall, providing access to the maxillary sinus ostium

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: During diagnostic nasal endoscopy, what structures are visualized on the 'third pass'?
back: The medial surface of the middle turbinate, leading to the sphenoethmoidal recess

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which sinus is most commonly (MC) involved in sinusitis in children vs adults?
back: Children: ethmoid sinus; Adults: maxillary sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which sinus is the most common site for mucocele and osteomyelitis?
back: The frontal sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which sinus is the most common cause of orbital complications from sinusitis?
back: The ethmoid sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which paranasal sinus is the first to be visible radiologically (in a newborn/infant)?
back: The maxillary sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which paranasal sinus is not present at birth?
back: The frontal sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Pott's puffy tumour?
back: A subperiosteal abscess (classically over the frontal bone) as a complication of frontal sinusitis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic imaging sign of invasive aspergillosis of the sinuses, its causative organism, and treatment?
back: 'Black turbinate' sign; caused by Aspergillus fumigatus; treated with Voriconazole

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic imaging sign of allergic fungal sinusitis, its characteristic nasal discharge, and treatment?
back: Double density sign on CT; peanut butter-like nasal discharge; treated with steroids

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the key features of mucormycosis ('black fungus') affecting the sinuses?
back: Causes ischaemic necrosis; it is angioinvasive

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the BENT-KUHN criteria for allergic fungal sinusitis, what are the five major criteria?
back: Type I hypersensitivity, nasal polyposis, characteristic CT findings, eosinophilic mucin without invasion, and a positive fungal stain

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: According to the BENT-KUHN criteria for allergic fungal sinusitis, what are the five minor criteria?
back: Asthma, unilateral disease, bone erosion, positive fungal cultures, Charcot-Leyden crystals, and serum eosinophilia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma (JNA), and in whom does it typically occur?
back: A vascular, androgen-dependent tumour typically occurring in adolescent males, presenting with epistaxis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for JNA, and from where does it typically originate?
back: Contrast-enhanced CT (CECT); originates from the sphenopalatine foramen

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What clinical sign is associated with JNA, and what does it indicate?
back: The Homan Miller sign - anterior bowing/displacement of the posterior wall of the maxilla, seen when the tumour fully occupies the pterygopalatine fossa

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which staging system is used for JNA, and is biopsy/FNAC recommended?
back: Radkowski staging is used; biopsy/FNAC is contraindicated (C/I) due to the vascular nature of the tumour

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Describe the Radkowski stages IA and IB of JNA.
back: IA: limited to the nose or nasopharynx; IB: extension into more than one paranasal sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Describe the Radkowski stages IIA, IIB, and IIC of JNA.
back: IIA: minimal extension through the sphenopalatine foramen; IIB: full occupation of the pterygopalatine fossa, displacing the posterior maxillary wall forward (Homan Miller sign); IIC: extension into the cheek and infratemporal fossa

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Describe the Radkowski stages IIIA and IIIB of JNA.
back: IIIA: erosion of the skull base with minimal intracranial extension; IIIB: extensive intracranial extension

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment approach for JNA?
back: Pre-operative embolisation of the maxillary artery, followed by surgery (endoscopic or open); intracranial extension is treated with radiotherapy (RT); flutamide (anti-androgen) may also be used

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is an antrochoanal polyp, is it unilateral or bilateral, what causes it, and how is it treated?
back: A nasal polyp that is unilateral (U/L); caused by infection; treated surgically

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is an ethmoidal polyp, is it unilateral or bilateral, what causes it, and how is it treated?
back: A nasal polyp that is bilateral (B/L); caused by allergy; treated medically

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Sampter's triad, and how is it classified immunologically?
back: Asthma + ethmoidal polyp + aspirin allergy; classified as non-allergic, non-IgE mediated

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the presenting features of choanal atresia?
back: Cyanosis and feeding difficulty

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is choanal atresia diagnosed, and what is the classic treatment?
back: Diagnosed by attempted catheter insertion which cannot cross the nares; treated with McGovern's technique, leading to definitive surgery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the indications for adenoidectomy?
back: Sleep apnea, chronic serous otitis media (combined with grommet insertion), and recurrent infections

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the contraindications for adenoidectomy?
back: Bleeding diathesis, acute infection, and velopharyngeal insufficiency

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the four components of Waldeyer's ring?
back: Nasal tonsils (adenoids), tubal tonsils, palatine tonsils, and lingual tonsils

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which virus is associated with nasopharyngeal (NP) carcinoma, and what dietary risk factor is classically implicated?
back: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV); Chinese food (containing nitrosamines) is a classic risk factor

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Is nasopharyngeal carcinoma radiosensitive, and what is its most common clinical presentation?
back: Yes, it is radiosensitive; most common clinical feature is cervical lymphadenopathy (LN - cervical)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Trotter's triad in nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
back: N - Neuralgia (via CN V); P - Palatal palsy (via CN X); C - Conductive hearing loss (CHL, due to serous otitis media)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is rhinolalia clausa, and what causes it?
back: A closed/muffled nasal voice; caused by adenoids or a nasopharyngeal mass with crowded teeth (nasal obstruction)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is rhinolalia aperta, and what causes it?
back: An open, hypernasal voice; caused by velopalatal insufficiency, such as cleft palate or palatal palsy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is inverted papilloma, and what is its alternate name?
back: Also called Ringertz tumour; a sinonasal tumour arising from the Schneiderian membrane

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In whom does inverted papilloma typically occur, what virus is it associated with, and what are the key clinical concerns?
back: Occurs in the elderly; associated with HPV; there is risk of recurrence and malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma (Sq.CC)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the characteristic imaging appearance of inverted papilloma?
back: A cerebriform appearance

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What surgical approach is known as the Moure incision?
back: Lateral rhinotomy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Weber-Ferguson incision used for?
back: Surgery for carcinoma of the maxillary sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What procedure is the Gluck-Sorensen incision used for?
back: Total laryngectomy (TL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Caldwell-Luc approach, and where is access to the maxillary sinus gained relative to the infraorbital foramen and malar buttress?
back: A surgical approach to the maxillary sinus via the canine fossa; the antrostomy area is located below the infraorbital foramen, near the malar buttress

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What occupational exposure is associated with sinonasal adenocarcinoma, and which is associated with squamous cell carcinoma?
back: Wood dust exposure is associated with adenocarcinoma; nickel exposure is associated with squamous cell carcinoma (Sq.CC)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Thudichum's speculum used for?
back: It is the anterior nasal speculum, used for examining the nasal cavity

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Killian's speculum used for?
back: A type of nasal speculum used in nasal/septal surgery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the Guerin sign, and in which Le Fort fracture is it seen?
back: Ecchymosis/haematoma in the greater palatine artery (GPA) area of the palate; seen in Le Fort I fracture

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the key features of a Le Fort I fracture?
back: Floating palate; Guerin sign (GPA haematoma) present; CSF rhinorrhoea is not typical

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the key features of a Le Fort II fracture?
back: A pyramidal fracture pattern, producing a 'floating maxilla'

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the key features of a Le Fort III fracture?
back: Craniofacial dysjunction/dysfunction, producing a 'floating face'

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is a tripod fracture, and which three sutures are involved?
back: A zygomaticomaxillary complex fracture involving the zygomaticofrontal suture, zygomaticotemporal suture, and zygomaticomaxillary suture

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is a longitudinal temporal bone fracture oriented relative to the petrous bone, and what are its typical features?
back: Parallel to the long axis of the petrous bone; causes conductive hearing loss, ossicular injury, and TM rupture; otic capsule involvement is rare

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is a transverse temporal bone fracture oriented relative to the petrous bone, and what are its typical features?
back: Perpendicular to the long axis of the petrous bone; causes sensorineural hearing loss and facial nerve paralysis; otic capsule involvement is more common

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What do the terms 'tear drop fracture', 'blow-out fracture', and 'trapdoor fracture' refer to, and what structure gets trapped?
back: All refer to a floor-of-orbit fracture, with the inferior rectus (IR) muscle becoming trapped

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the most common (MC) site of a CSF rhinorrhoea leak?
back: The cribriform plate

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) to biochemically confirm a fluid is CSF?
back: Beta-2 transferrin testing

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) to confirm the anatomical site of a CSF leak?
back: CT is superior to MRI cisternography for site localization; heavily T2-weighted MRI is also used

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is a septal hematoma, what symptom does it cause, and how is it treated?
back: A collection of blood under the nasal septal mucoperichondrium; causes bilateral nasal obstruction; treated by Incision & Drainage (I&D)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In nasal septal fracture lines, what are the horizontal and vertical fracture lines called?
back: Horizontal fracture line: Jarjavay line; Vertical fracture line: Chevallet line

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In management of a nasal bone fracture, when should closed reduction be performed if there is no edema, and what should be done if edema is present?
back: Closed reduction (CR) should be done immediately if there is no edema; if edema is present, wait 7 days before reduction

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is deviated nasal septum (DNS) clinically tested for?
back: The Cottle test

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the most common cause (MCC) of deviated nasal septum?
back: Birth trauma

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: At what minimum age is DNS surgically managed, and what are the two surgical options?
back: ≥16 years of age; managed by SMR (submucous resection) or septoplasty

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Compare the 2-flap (Killian's incision) and 1-flap (Freer's incision) techniques for septal surgery in terms of recurrence and perforation risk.
back: 2 flaps (Killian's incision): lower recurrence, higher perforation risk. 1 flap (Freer's incision): higher recurrence, lower perforation risk

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which forceps are classically used for reducing nasal bone fractures?
back: Asch forceps and Walsham forceps

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What condition causes a 'Tapir nose' or 'Hebra nose' appearance, and what is its causative organism?
back: Rhinoscleroma, caused by the Frisch bacillus (Klebsiella rhinoscleromatis)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What histological findings are characteristic of rhinoscleroma?
back: Russell bodies and Mikulicz cells

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the drug of choice (DOC) for rhinoscleroma?
back: Antibiotics - fluoroquinolones (FQ) or aminoglycosides (Ag)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What condition is nicknamed 'swimmer's disease' and produces a mulberry/strawberry-like nose appearance, and what is its causative organism?
back: Rhinosporidiosis, caused by Rhinosporidium seeberi (an aquatic protozoan)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic histopathological (H/P) finding in rhinosporidiosis?
back: Sporangium containing endospores

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment of choice (TOC) for rhinosporidiosis?
back: Excision plus Dapsone

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What condition presents with a roomy nasal cavity and 'merciful anosmia', and what is its causative organism?
back: Atrophic rhinitis, caused by Klebsiella ozaenae (Perez bacillus)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the medical management for atrophic rhinitis, and which surgical procedure is contraindicated?
back: Alkaline nasal douching; Young's operation is contraindicated

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Lautenslager's procedure used for?
back: Submucous Teflon injection, used in the management of atrophic rhinitis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the composition of the alkaline nasal douche solution?
back: 2 parts sodium chloride (NaCl) + 1 part sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) + 1 part sodium biborate

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which conditions can cause septal perforation involving cartilage more than bone?
back: Cocaine use (C>B - cartilage more than bone)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which conditions can cause septal perforation involving cartilage only, and which involve both cartilage and bone?
back: Cartilage only: TB, Sarcoidosis, SLE, Leprosy. Both cartilage and bone: Syphilis, Wegener's granulomatosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What causes rhinophyma, and with which skin condition is it associated?
back: Sebaceous gland hypertrophy; strongly associated with rosacea

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the underlying mechanism of vasomotor rhinitis, and what is its treatment?
back: Parasympathetic activation; treated with topical steroids, or vidian neurectomy (targeting GSPN + deep petrosal nerve) if refractory

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is rhinitis medicamentosa, what causes it, and how is it treated?
back: Rebound nasal congestion due to prolonged use of decongestants, causing rebound vasodilation; treated by stopping the offending drug

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are Dennie-Morgan folds and allergic shiners signs of?
back: Allergic rhinitis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the most common cause (MCC) of anterior epistaxis, and in which age group is it more common?
back: Trauma; more common in children

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which anatomical site and vascular plexus are involved in anterior epistaxis?
back: Little's area, involving the Kiesselbach plexus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which vein is classically implicated in anterior epistaxis in adults?
back: The retrocolumellar vein

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which venous plexus is involved in posterior epistaxis?
back: Woodruff's venous plexus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which artery is known as the 'artery of epistaxis'?
back: The sphenopalatine artery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In modern epistaxis management, what is preferred over nasal packing?
back: Nasal endoscopy and cautery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does TESPAL stand for, and when is it used?
back: Trans-nasal Endoscopic SphenoPalatine Artery Ligation; used as definitive surgical management for refractory/posterior epistaxis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the recommended first-aid technique for epistaxis?
back: Sit up slightly, lean forward, tilt the head forward, and pinch the tip of the nose with two fingers for 15-20 minutes

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Ludwig's angina, and what characteristic finding is seen on examination?
back: A rapidly spreading cellulitis of the floor of the mouth; presents with a 'double tongue' appearance (tongue pushed upward and backward)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Vincent's angina, and what is its alternate name?
back: Also called 'trench mouth'; caused by poor dental hygiene

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Vincent's angina also known as clinically, and which organisms cause it?
back: Acute Necrotising Ulcerative Gingivostomatitis (ANUG); caused by Fusobacterium and Treponema (fusospirochetal infection)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the key clinical features of acute epiglottitis?
back: Fever, respiratory distress, drooling, and the patient adopts the tripod position

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the most common cause (MCC) of acute epiglottitis, and what is the management approach?
back: Streptococcus is more common than Haemophilus influenzae; management is airway management first, followed by antibiotics

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic X-ray sign of acute epiglottitis?
back: The thumb sign

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What virus most commonly causes croup (acute laryngotracheobronchitis, LTB), and what are its key clinical features?
back: Parainfluenza virus; presents with biphasic stridor and a barking cough

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the classic X-ray sign of croup?
back: The steeple sign

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment approach for croup?
back: Racemic epinephrine, followed by dexamethasone

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What type of stridor is produced by supraglottic obstruction?
back: Inspiratory stridor

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What type of stridor is produced by glottic and subglottic obstruction?
back: Biphasic stridor

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What type of stridor is produced by intrathoracic airway obstruction?
back: Expiratory stridor

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the classic chest X-ray findings in bronchiolitis?
back: Increased bronchovascular markings and hyperinflation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the most common cause (MCC) of bronchiolitis, and what is its main clinical symptom?
back: Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV); presents with wheeze

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which drugs are used for high-risk prophylaxis against RSV bronchiolitis, and what do they target?
back: Palivizumab and Nirsevimab; both target the RSV F-protein

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment for RSV bronchiolitis?
back: Ribavirin

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: A 'staccato cough' in an infant is characteristic of which infection?
back: Chlamydial pneumonia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Name the arteries that contribute to the blood supply of the tonsil.
back: Maxillary artery, descending palatine artery, ascending pharyngeal artery (via tonsillar branches), ascending palatine artery, facial artery (via the tonsillar artery), and lingual artery (via dorsal linguae branches) - all ultimately from the external carotid artery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the 'recurrent infection' criterion for tonsillectomy based on episode frequency?
back: ≥3 episodes/year for 3 consecutive years, OR ≥5 episodes/year for 2 consecutive years, OR ≥7 episodes in a single year

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Besides recurrent infection, what other indication warrants tonsillectomy?
back: Obstructive symptoms, such as sleep apnea

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is 'primary' tonsillectomy-related hemorrhage, and when does it occur?
back: Hemorrhage occurring intra-operatively

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is 'reactionary' tonsillectomy-related hemorrhage, when does it occur, what is its source, and how is it managed?
back: Occurs within ≤24 hours post-op; the source is typically the paratonsillar vein, due to slippage of a ligature; managed by re-exploration

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is 'secondary' tonsillectomy-related hemorrhage, when does it occur, and what causes it?
back: Occurs 24 hours to 10-14 days post-op, due to infection; managed with antibiotics

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is a peritonsillar abscess also called, and what clinical sign does it produce?
back: Also called quinsy; produces uvula deviation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is a peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) treated?
back: Incision and drainage (I&D)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What clinical sign is associated with a parapharyngeal space abscess?
back: Neck swelling

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What instrument and positioning are used for tonsillectomy?
back: The Boyle-Davis mouth gag, mounted on a Draffin bipod stand, with the patient in the Rose position with the neck extended

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Grisel syndrome, and with what condition is it associated?
back: Atlanto-axial dislocation; associated with achondroplasia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: At what vertebral level is the prevertebral space located, and which fascia bounds it?
back: At the level of T3-T4; bounded by the prevertebral fascia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the 'danger space' in the neck, which fascia bounds it, and what is its inferior extent?
back: A potential space bounded by the alar fascia; it extends inferiorly down to the diaphragm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the inferior extent of the retropharyngeal (RP) space, and which fascia bounds it?
back: Extends to the level of T4; bounded by the buccopharyngeal fascia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How does a prevertebral abscess compare to a retropharyngeal (RP) abscess in terms of extent/severity?
back: A prevertebral abscess is noted to be more extensive/severe than an RP abscess

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What are the layers of the pharyngeal wall, from innermost to outermost?
back: Mucous membrane, pharyngobasilar fascia, muscular coat, and buccopharyngeal fascia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is Killian's dehiscence, and what condition arises from it?
back: A weak area between the thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus muscles of the inferior constrictor; it is the site of origin of Zenker's diverticulum

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which nerve supplies the thyropharyngeus muscle, and which nerve supplies the cricopharyngeus muscle?
back: Thyropharyngeus: External laryngeal nerve (ELN); Cricopharyngeus: Recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the foramen of Morgagni, and what structures pass through it?
back: A gap in the pharyngeal wall through which the auditory (Eustachian) tube and tensor palatini muscle pass

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: On direct laryngoscopy from superior to inferior, name the structures typically seen.
back: Vallecula, aryepiglottic fold (AEF), true vocal cord (VC), and the pyriform fossa

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Injury within the pyriform fossa classically affects which nerve?
back: The internal laryngeal nerve (ILN)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: At what age does respiratory papillomatosis typically present, and what causes it?
back: Ages 6-11 years; caused by HPV types 6 and 11

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: How is respiratory papillomatosis typically acquired, and how is it treated?
back: Acquired via HPV warts during vaginal delivery; treated with CO2 laser

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is laryngomalacia, what is its classic appearance, what symptom does it cause, and how is it managed?
back: A condition with an 'omega-shaped' epiglottis; causes inspiratory stridor; managed with reassurance (usually self-resolving)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What causes Reinke's edema?
back: Smoking and GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is a 'turban' or 'rat-bitten' epiglottis appearance seen in, and what is the associated pain level?
back: TB epiglottitis; presents with intermediate pain

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Are vocal nodules typically unilateral or bilateral, and how are they treated?
back: Bilateral (B/L); treated with voice rest

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Are vocal polyps typically unilateral or bilateral, and how are they treated?
back: Unilateral (U/L); treated with microlaryngeal surgery (MLS)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: List the five structures not visible on indirect laryngoscopy.
back: Vestibule, epiglottis (laryngeal surface), subglottic area, posterior cricoid area, and the apex of the piriform fossa

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What condition produces a 'keyhole' appearance of the vocal cords?
back: Phonasthenia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is pachyderma laryngitis also known as, and what is its characteristic appearance?
back: Also called contact ulcer; has a 'cobblestone' appearance

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T1 laryngeal carcinoma?
back: One subsite involved, with a mobile vocal cord (VC)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T2 laryngeal carcinoma?
