back: Two subsites involved, with reduced vocal cord motility

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T3 laryngeal carcinoma?
back: A fixed vocal cord; involvement of the '3P' spaces - preglottic, paraglottic, or postcricoid - or invasion of the inner cortex of the thyroid cartilage

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T4a laryngeal carcinoma?
back: Complete erosion of the thyroid cartilage cortex

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T4b laryngeal carcinoma?
back: Very advanced disease with invasion of the internal carotid artery (ICA), mediastinum, or prevertebral space

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which subsite of laryngeal carcinoma is most common, and which has the best prognosis?
back: Glottic carcinoma is both the most common subsite and has the best prognosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for staging laryngeal carcinoma, and which for cartilage invasion?
back: Staging: CECT; Cartilage invasion: MRI

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What intra-operative staining technique is used in laryngeal cancer surgery, and what is the stain?
back: Supravital staining using Toluidine blue

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment for T1 glottic carcinoma?
back: CO2 laser excision

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment approach for T1/T2 laryngeal carcinoma?
back: Radiotherapy (RT) is preferred over partial laryngectomy (PL)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the treatment approach for T3/T4 laryngeal carcinoma?
back: Total laryngectomy (TL) is preferred over chemoradiotherapy (CT-RT)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What voice restoration options are available after total laryngectomy?
back: Bloom-Singer's tracheoesophageal prosthesis, and the electrolarynx (an external device)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is a Montgomery T-tube used for?
back: As an airway stent, post-surgery

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the purpose of the super-supraglottic swallow technique?
back: To reduce the risk of aspiration (used after partial laryngectomy for swallowing rehabilitation)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T1 oral cavity cancer?
back: Tumor ≤2 cm, with depth of invasion (DOI) ≤5 mm (note: DOI, not tumor thickness)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T2 oral cavity cancer?
back: Tumor 2-4 cm, OR DOI 5-10 mm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T3 oral cavity cancer?
back: Tumor >4 cm, OR DOI >10 mm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T4a oral cavity cancer (moderately advanced local disease)?
back: For lip tumors: invasion through cortical bone, or involvement of the inferior alveolar nerve, floor of mouth, skin of face, or maxillary sinus

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What defines T4b oral cavity cancer (very advanced local disease)?
back: Invasion of the skull base, encasement of the internal carotid artery, or invasion of the masticator space or pterygoid plates

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for oral cavity cancer in general, and specifically for tongue cancer?
back: General oral cavity cancer: CECT; Tongue cancer: MRI

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: Which has higher malignant potential - erythroplakia or leukoplakia?
back: Erythroplakia has higher malignant potential than leukoplakia

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What is the concept of 'field cancerization' in oral cavity cancer?
back: The concept that the entire exposed mucosal field (e.g., from tobacco/alcohol exposure) is at risk of developing multiple independent malignant lesions

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does the COMMANDO procedure stand for?
back: COMbined MAndibulectomy and Neck Dissection Operation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In nodal staging for oral cavity cancer, what defines N1?
back: A single ipsilateral (I/L) node, less than 3 cm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In nodal staging for oral cavity cancer, what defines N2a?
back: A single ipsilateral node, 3-6 cm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In nodal staging for oral cavity cancer, what defines N2b?
back: Multiple ipsilateral nodes, each less than 6 cm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In nodal staging for oral cavity cancer, what defines N2c?
back: Contralateral or bilateral nodes, each less than 6 cm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: In nodal staging for oral cavity cancer, what defines N3?
back: Any single node greater than 6 cm

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
front: What does ENE+ mean in nodal staging, and what is its prognostic significance?
back: Extranodal extension positive; it indicates a poor prognosis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In Modified Radical Neck Dissection (MRND) classification, which structure is preserved in Type I?
back: The Spinal Accessory Nerve (SAN)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In Modified Radical Neck Dissection (MRND) classification, which structures are preserved in Type II?
back: The Spinal Accessory Nerve (SAN) and the Internal Jugular Vein (IJV)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In Modified Radical Neck Dissection (MRND) classification, which structures are preserved in Type III?
back: The Spinal Accessory Nerve (SAN), Internal Jugular Vein (IJV), and Sternocleidomastoid muscle (SCM) - this is the 'functional' neck dissection

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What glandular structures are typically removed during neck dissection?
back: Lymph nodes of levels I-V, the submandibular gland (SMG), and the tail of the parotid gland

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which gland is retained (not routinely removed) during neck dissection?
back: The sublingual gland

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which incision is classically used for neck dissection?
back: The Modified Schobinger's incision

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which lymph node levels are removed in a supra-omohyoid neck dissection?
back: Levels I, II, and III

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which lymph node level corresponds to the central compartment / Delphian lymph node?
back: Level VI

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the most common site for sialolithiasis (salivary gland stones)?
back: The submandibular duct (Wharton's duct)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which grading system is used for salivary gland tumors?
back: The MILAN system

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the most common benign salivary gland tumor (and most common salivary gland tumor overall)?
back: Pleomorphic adenoma (Benign Mixed Tumor, BMT)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the most common malignant salivary gland tumor?
back: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which salivary gland tumor is classically associated with perineural invasion?
back: Adenoid cystic carcinoma

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In which patient population does Warthin's tumor typically occur, and what is characteristic on Tc99 pertechnetate scan?
back: Occurs in elderly smokers; shows a 'hot spot' on Tc99 pertechnetate scan

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the histological appearance of Warthin's tumor?
back: Papillary cystadenolymphomatous pattern - lymphoid tissue in the stroma with oncocytic (pink granular cytoplasm) epithelial cells

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the classic radical neck dissection (removing SAN, IJV, and SCM together) also known as?
back: Crile's radical neck dissection

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: All intrinsic laryngeal muscles are supplied by which nerve, with one exception - which muscle and nerve is the exception?
back: All are supplied by the Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve (RLN), except the cricothyroid muscle, which is supplied by the external branch of the Superior Laryngeal Nerve (SLN)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the function of the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN), and what mnemonic describes its action on the vocal cords?
back: Motor supply to the cricothyroid muscle; mnemonic TAD - Tensor and Adductor of the vocal cords

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the function of the internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (SLN), and what structure does it pierce?
back: Sensory supply to the supraglottis; it pierces the thyrohyoid membrane

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Damage to which nerve impairs the cough reflex?
back: The Superior Laryngeal Nerve (SLN)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which laryngeal muscle is the only abductor of the vocal cords, and why is it called the 'safety muscle'?
back: The posterior cricoarytenoid muscle; it is called the safety muscle because it is the only muscle that opens (abducts) the airway

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which laryngeal muscle acts as a relaxer of the vocal cords?
back: The vocalis muscle

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the clinical effect of internal laryngeal nerve (ILN) palsy?
back: Aspiration

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the clinical effect of external laryngeal nerve (ELN) palsy?
back: Loss of cricothyroid (tensor) function, causing inability to change pitch

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the clinical effect of complete superior laryngeal nerve (SLN) palsy?
back: Aspiration plus inability to change pitch

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the clinical effect of recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) palsy?
back: Stridor, potentially requiring tracheostomy; bilateral RLN palsy may be managed with the Kashima procedure (post-cordotomy)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the clinical effect of complete (combined SLN + RLN) nerve palsy, and what vocal cord position results?
back: Aspiration plus aphonia; the vocal cord rests in the cadaveric/paramedian position

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In the vocal cord position table, what distance from the midline and clinical scenario corresponds to the 'median' position?
back: 0 mm distance; seen during speech/swallowing

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In the vocal cord position table, what distance from the midline and clinical scenario corresponds to the 'paramedian' position?
back: 1.5 mm distance; seen in RLN palsy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In the vocal cord position table, what distance from the midline and clinical scenario corresponds to the 'intermediate/cadaveric' position?
back: 3.5 mm distance; seen in complete paralysis

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In the vocal cord position table, what distance from the midline and clinical scenario corresponds to 'slight abduction'?
back: 7 mm distance; seen in normal respiration

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In the vocal cord position table, what distance from the midline and clinical scenario corresponds to 'full abduction'?
back: 9.5 mm distance; seen in strained respiration

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is dysphonia plica ventricularis?
back: A low-pitch voice produced by using the false vocal cords (ventricular folds) for phonation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is Type I (Isshiki) thyroplasty, and what is it used for?
back: Medialisation thyroplasty; used to treat unilateral vocal cord palsy

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is Type II (Isshiki) thyroplasty, and what is it associated with?
back: Lateralisation thyroplasty; associated with bilateral RLN palsy (B/L RLN#) and the Guttzmann manoeuver

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is Type III (Isshiki) thyroplasty, and what voice condition is it used to treat?
back: Shortening/relaxation thyroplasty; used to treat puberphonia (changing the voice from a female-sounding pitch to a male-sounding pitch, F to M)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is Type IV (Isshiki) thyroplasty, and what voice condition is it used to treat?
back: Lengthening/tightening thyroplasty; used to treat androphonia (changing the voice from a male-sounding pitch to a female-sounding pitch, M to F)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is a dermoid cyst, and where is it classically located?
back: A compressible congenital cyst, classically located at the frontonasal or frontozygomatic suture

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is characteristic of an epidermal/sebaceous cyst on examination?
back: The presence of a punctum

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What are the examination findings of a cystic hygroma?
back: Compressible, and transillumination positive

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: In the differential diagnosis of a midline nasal swelling, how is a meningocele distinguished from a glioma?
back: A meningocele is compressible and shows a positive Furstenberg sign (increases in size with jugular vein compression/crying); a glioma is non-compressible

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is a carotid body tumour (paraganglioma), and what is its characteristic MRI appearance?
back: A tumour arising at the carotid bifurcation, showing a 'salt and pepper' appearance on MRI

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What angiographic sign and genetic mutation are associated with carotid body tumour?
back: Lyre's sign (splaying of the carotid bifurcation); associated with SDH gene mutation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is a laryngocoele, in whom is it classically seen, and what clinical sign is positive?
back: An air-filled dilatation of the laryngeal saccule; classically seen in trumpet players ('trumpet blower'); Bryce sign is positive

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is a (simple) ranula, where does it arise, and how is it treated?
back: A mucous extravasation cyst arising from the sublingual (SL) gland; treated by excision

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is a plunging ranula, what type of cyst is it, and how is it treated?
back: A ranula that extends from the sublingual to the submandibular space (SL-SM); it is a mucus retention cyst; treated by marsupialisation

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What type of skin incision is used for an emergency tracheostomy versus an elective tracheostomy?
back: Emergency tracheostomy: vertical incision; Elective tracheostomy: horizontal incision

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the purpose of a cuffed tracheostomy tube?
back: Airway protection

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the most common (MC) site/level for tracheostomy placement?
back: The 2nd-3rd tracheal ring

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: When is a 'high' tracheostomy performed, and when is a 'low' tracheostomy performed?
back: High tracheostomy: performed for carcinoma of the larynx (to keep the tumor margin clear); Low tracheostomy: performed for papillomatosis/infection

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: How is partial tracheostomy tube blockage managed, and how is complete blockage managed?
back: Partial blockage: suctioning; Complete blockage: removal (and replacement) of the tube

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Where is the narrowest part of the larynx located in children, and at what vertebral level?
back: At the subglottis (cricoid cartilage level), at the level of C2-C3

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Where is the narrowest part of the larynx located in adults, and at what vertebral level?
back: At the glottis (vocal cords), at the level of C3-C6

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: Which staging system is used for subglottic stenosis?
back: Cotton-Meyer staging

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What percentage of obstruction defines Cotton-Meyer Grade I and Grade II subglottic stenosis?
back: Grade I: 0-50% obstruction; Grade II: 50-70% obstruction

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What percentage of obstruction defines Cotton-Meyer Grade III and Grade IV subglottic stenosis?
back: Grade III: 70-99% obstruction; Grade IV: no lumen (complete obstruction)

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is the treatment for subglottic stenosis?
back: Balloon dilatation plus Mitomycin C

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What is ciliocytophoria, and what causes it?
back: A finding of ciliary damage, associated with viral infection

subject: ENT (Otorhinolaryngology)
deck: Part-13: Neck Dissection, VC Palsy, Masses & Misc
front: What are the three extrinsic membranes of the larynx?
back: Hyoepiglottic membrane, thyrohyoid membrane, and cricotracheal membrane
